steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction/choledocholithiasis, post-operative complication (biloma, abscess, bowel obstruction), alcoholic hepatitis flare
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the markedly elevated liver enzymes (AST 264, ALT 116, ALP 282), hyperbilirubinemia (total bili 2.1), and post-cholecystectomy abdominal pain reflect a biliary ductal process (retained stone, stricture, bile leak/biloma) versus hepatic parenchymal disease versus a post-operative intra-abdominal complication
ensemble.expected_finding: Biliary ductal dilation (common bile duct >6-7 mm), intrahepatic ductal dilation, a retained/recurrent stone, bile leak/biloma, or hepatic parenchymal changes consistent with alcoholic hepatitis; alternatively, free fluid or an abscess near the operative bed
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The combination of post-cholecystectomy pain, bilious vomiting, significantly elevated cholestatic and hepatocellular enzymes, hyperbilirubinemia, and recent open cholecystectomy makes biliary ductal pathology or a post-operative hepatobiliary complication the leading concern, and abdominal ultrasound is the first-line, non-invasive modality to evaluate the biliary tree and liver in this setting.
grounding: History: 'laparoscopic converted to open cholecystectomy'
History: 'significant per-incisional pain as well as inability to tolerate oral intake'
History: 'a dozen episodes of bilious emesis'
History: 'gallstone pancreatitis s/p stent placement/removal and sphincterotomy'
Labs: 'Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 264.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)'
Labs: 'Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT): 116.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)'
Labs: 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 282.0 IU/L (ref 40.0-130.0)'
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 2.1 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5)'
Labs: 'Lipase: 26.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — pancreatitis less likely
Labs: 'Platelet Count: 66.0 K/uL' and 'INR(PT): 1.4' — consistent with chronic liver disease/portal hypertension
Urine: 'Urobilinogen: 8.0 mg/dL (ref 0.2-1.0)' — elevated, supporting hepatobiliary dysfunction
ensemble.reasoning: This post-cholecystectomy patient with bilious vomiting, peri-incisional pain, markedly elevated transaminases and alkaline phosphatase, hyperbilirubinemia, and a history of recurrent biliary disease (gallstone pancreatitis, prior ERCP with sphincterotomy) raises strong concern for a biliary ductal complication such as retained stone, bile duct injury/stricture, or bile leak. Lipase is normal, making acute pancreatitis less likely. A right upper quadrant/liver ultrasound is the logical first imaging step to assess biliary ductal caliber, hepatic parenchyma, and the operative bed for fluid collections before considering MRCP or CT.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction/choledocholithiasis, post-operative complication (biloma, abscess, bowel obstruction), alcoholic hepatitis flare
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: Whether the markedly elevated liver enzymes (AST 264, ALT 116, ALP 282), hyperbilirubinemia (total bili 2.1), and post-cholecystectomy abdominal pain reflect a biliary ductal process (retained stone, stricture, bile leak/biloma) versus hepatic parenchymal disease versus a post-operative intra-abdominal complication
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Biliary ductal dilation (common bile duct >6-7 mm), intrahepatic ductal dilation, a retained/recurrent stone, bile leak/biloma, or hepatic parenchymal changes consistent with alcoholic hepatitis; alternatively, free fluid or an abscess near the operative bed
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The combination of post-cholecystectomy pain, bilious vomiting, significantly elevated cholestatic and hepatocellular enzymes, hyperbilirubinemia, and recent open cholecystectomy makes biliary ductal pathology or a post-operative hepatobiliary complication the leading concern, and abdominal ultrasound is the first-line, non-invasive modality to evaluate the biliary tree and liver in this setting.

verification.actual_finding: The ultrasound showed a mildly prominent common bile duct at 6 mm (post-cholecystectomy), slightly heterogeneous hepatic echotexture, an avascular hypoechoic focus in the left hepatic lobe (~1.0 cm), no intrahepatic ductal dilation, and no perihepatic free fluid.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The mildly prominent CBD (6 mm) and heterogeneous hepatic parenchyma partially match the expected biliary ductal process and hepatic parenchymal changes, while the absence of intrahepatic dilation, retained stone, biloma, or free fluid narrows but does not fully resolve the differential between biliary and parenchymal disease.

steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Post-operative complication (bowel obstruction, anastomotic/wound complication, intra-abdominal abscess, biloma, or bile duct injury); choledocholithiasis/biliary obstruction; alcoholic hepatitis flare with hepatic pathology (given ill-defined hypoechoic hepatic lesion on US); small bowel obstruction
ensemble.information_gap: The ultrasound is limited and cannot characterize the intra-abdominal anatomy adequately in a post-operative patient with bilious vomiting, peri-incisional pain, elevated LFTs/ALP/bilirubin, thrombocytopenia, and an unexplained hypoechoic hepatic lesion — the physician needs cross-sectional imaging to identify a structural post-operative complication or biliary pathology that ultrasound cannot resolve.
ensemble.expected_finding: A post-operative complication such as small bowel obstruction (transition point, dilated loops), intra-abdominal fluid collection/abscess, biloma, or bile duct injury; alternatively, biliary ductal dilation with choledocholithiasis, or further characterization of the hepatic hypoechoic lesion (abscess vs. other); or evidence of ileus vs. mechanical obstruction explaining the bilious emesis.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The ultrasound is limited and non-diagnostic in a post-cholecystectomy patient with bilious vomiting, peri-incisional pain, markedly elevated AST/ALT/ALP, hyperbilirubinemia, thrombocytopenia, and an unexplained hepatic lesion — CT with contrast is the appropriate next step to survey the entire abdomen and pelvis for post-operative complications and biliary pathology.
grounding: History: 'recent laparoscopic converted to open cholecystectomy' and 'post-operatively patient had multiple hospital visits'
History: 'significant per-incisional pain as well as inability to tolerate oral intake' and 'a dozen episodes of bilious emesis'
History: 'pain mostly around his umbilical incision site'
Labs: AST 264 IU/L (ref 0-40), ALT 116 IU/L (ref 0-40), ALP 282 IU/L (ref 40-130), Bilirubin Total 2.1 mg/dL (ref 0-1.5)
Labs: Platelet Count 66.0 K/uL (ref 150-400), INR 1.4 (ref 0.9-1.1), PT 15.1 sec (ref 9.4-12.5)
Prior imaging: 'ill-defined hypoechoic focus within the left hepatic lobe...avascular, without a correlate on the CT'
Prior imaging: 'The pancreas is poorly visualized' and 'Limited right upper quadrant ultrasound'
Prior imaging: 'common duct is mildly prominent and measures 6 mm in keeping with history of cholecystectomy'
Physical exam: 'Soft, mildly distended, incision site clean/dry/intact. Mild epigastric pain along ___ incision site'
ensemble.reasoning: This post-cholecystectomy patient presents with bilious vomiting, peri-incisional pain, markedly elevated hepatocellular and cholestatic liver enzymes, hyperbilirubinemia, thrombocytopenia, and a coagulopathy — raising concern for a post-operative complication (bowel obstruction, biloma, bile duct injury, or intra-abdominal abscess) that the limited and technically inadequate ultrasound cannot exclude. The unexplained hypoechoic hepatic lesion without CT correlate and the poorly visualized pancreas on ultrasound leave critical diagnostic gaps that only cross-sectional CT with contrast can address. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the logical next step to survey the entire operative field and characterize both the hepatic lesion and any biliary or bowel pathology.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Post-operative complication (bowel obstruction, anastomotic/wound complication, intra-abdominal abscess, biloma, or bile duct injury); choledocholithiasis/biliary obstruction; alcoholic hepatitis flare with hepatic pathology (given ill-defined hypoechoic hepatic lesion on US); small bowel obstruction
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The ultrasound is limited and cannot characterize the intra-abdominal anatomy adequately in a post-operative patient with bilious vomiting, peri-incisional pain, elevated LFTs/ALP/bilirubin, thrombocytopenia, and an unexplained hypoechoic hepatic lesion — the physician needs cross-sectional imaging to identify a structural post-operative complication or biliary pathology that ultrasound cannot resolve.
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: A post-operative complication such as small bowel obstruction (transition point, dilated loops), intra-abdominal fluid collection/abscess, biloma, or bile duct injury; alternatively, biliary ductal dilation with choledocholithiasis, or further characterization of the hepatic hypoechoic lesion (abscess vs. other); or evidence of ileus vs. mechanical obstruction explaining the bilious emesis.

verification.actual_finding: CT showed massively enlarged liver (28 cm) with hypodense attenuation and perihepatic stranding suggesting hepatitis/advanced liver disease, splenomegaly, non-dilated CBD, no biloma, no biliary ductal dilation, no bowel obstruction, no abscess, no intra-abdominal fluid collection, and an incidental finding of a thickened/dilated appendix concerning for early appendicitis.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The CT did not reveal any of the expected post-operative structural complications (SBO, biloma, bile duct injury, choledocholithiasis, ductal dilation, or abscess), instead pointing toward intrinsic hepatic parenchymal disease and splenomegaly as the underlying pathology.
